coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and application of fluorescent N,S co-doped carbon dots based on on-off-on quenching mode for the collaboration detection of iron ions and ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Yujing
Guo, Jiaqing
Deng, Yanhua
Weng, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method using oleic acid as the medium, ʟ-cystine and citric acid monohydrate as precursors. Based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence quenching mode, a novel method was established for determination of both Fe3+ and ascorbic acid. The synthesized NS-CDs can be employed as fluorescence chemical sensors for the direct determination of free iron in the aqueous phase and indirect determination of the ascorbic acid contents of vitamin C tablets with linear ranges of 0–10 μM (n = 3) and 0–30 μM (n = 3), and detection limits of 36.6 and 102.5 nM, respectively. These results demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits good selectivity and linearity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Collaboration Detection
Iron Ions
Ascorbic Acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield
Detection of Fe3+ and AA using the NS-CDs
Determination of the Fe3+ and AA contents using the NS-CDs
Sensitivity of the NS-CDs to the detection of Fe3+ and AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM (A),size distribution(B), HRTEM (C) and XRD(D) spectrum of the obtained NS-CDs.
Characterization of the NS-CDs by XPS: (A) The full spectrum, (B) the carbon spectrum, (C) the nitrogen spectrum, and (D) the sulfur spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the NS-CDs. The inset shows the solution at fluorescence (left) and at 365-nm irradiation (right). (B) The wavelength-dependent fluorescence spectra.
Fluorescence signal response time upon the addition of Fe3+ and AA.
Effect of the solution pH on the fluorescence quenching by (A) AA and (B) Fe3+, and (C) on the fluorescence recovery upon the addition of AA.

Effects of common ions on (A) the fluorescence of the NS-CDs, (B) the fluorescence quenching of the NS-CDs + Fe3+ system, (C) the fluorescence quenching of the NS-CDs + AA system, and (D) the fluorescence recovery of the NS-CDs + Fe3+ + AA system.

Effects of different amino acids on the fluorescence recovery of the NS-CDs + Fe3+ system (irradiation at 365 nm): Fe3+, Fe3+ + AA, Fe3+ + Gly, Fe3+ + Val, Fe3+ + Phe, Fe3+ + Ala, Fe3+ + His, and Fe3+ + Trp.
(A) Effect of the Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity. (B) Plot of the Fe3+ concentration versus the fluorescence quenching.
(A) Effect of the AA concentration on the NS-CDs + Fe3+ fluorescence intensity. (B) Plot of the AA concentration versus the NS-CDs + Fe3+ fluorescence recovery rate.
Determination of the Fe3+ concentrations in water samples.
Determination of the AA concentrations of vitamin C tablets.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of King Saud University.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and application of fluorescent N,S co-doped carbon dots based on on-off-on quenching mode for the collaboration detection of iron ions and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, Fujian, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method using oleic acid as the medium, ʟ-cystine and citric acid monohydrate as precursors. Based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence quenching mode, a novel method was established for determination of both Fe3+ and ascorbic acid. The synthesized NS-CDs can be employed as fluorescence chemical sensors for the direct determination of free iron in the aqueous phase and indirect determination of the ascorbic acid contents of vitamin C tablets with linear ranges of 0–10 μM (n = 3) and 0–30 μM (n = 3), and detection limits of 36.6 and 102.5 nM, respectively. These results demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits good selectivity and linearity.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an indispensable trace element in the body, mainly present in the form of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in hemoglobin. Therefore, it is involved in aerobic metabolism and biological electron transfer due to its susceptibility to redox reactions and its strong affinity for oxygen [1–3]. However, excess amounts of iron in the body can cause tissue damage, organ failure, and eventually death [4]. There are many methods to determine iron ion content, including atomic absorption spectroscopy [5], electron paramagnetic resonance [6], mass spectrometry [7], inductively-coupled plasma mass spectroscopy,[8], and electrochemical methods [9]. However, these methods have certain drawbacks, such as the difficulty of probe preparation, the complexity of sample preparation and the tedious operation.[10] Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple, rapid and efficient method for the determination of Fe3+.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is an essential vitamin for humans. Healthy adults need about 1500 mg of AA (up to 3000 mg) to maintain normal physiological function. AA is important because it is a highly effective antioxidant, which can remove reactive oxygen species and other free radicals from the body, thereby playing a special role in redox reactions [11], and protecting cells from oxidative damage. It has wide medical applications, such as in the treatment of colds and metal poisoning, as well as cancer prevention [12,13]. However, although AA is an important vitamin, excess or deficiency of AA can lead to disease. Thus, when supplementing natural or fortified foods and medications with AA, a suggested dosage should be applied. Developing a simple and fast method for the determination of AA content in foods and pharmaceuticals is important [14–16].

Fluorescence-based chemical sensors offer several advantages over other detection methods, including high sensitivity, high selectivity, and real-time rapid response monitoring [17]. Fluorescent carbon dots, also known as fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, are an example of a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials [18,19]. Compared to conventional organic small-molecule fluorescent substances, this nanomaterial exhibits adjustable emission wavelength, high stability, facile surface functionalization, and strong photobleaching resistance [20]. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles include graphene quantum dots, fluorescent polymer fluorescent carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, and fluorescent carbon nitride. In order to improve the properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDS), many researches focus on the modification of carbon quantum dots with sulfur or nitrogen atoms [21,22]. So far, such nanoparticles have been widely employed in modern medicine, chemical engineering, biological analysis, and environmental testing, and show potential for further developments.

In the present study, we designed a rapid and economical solvothermal method for the synthesis of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon Dots (NS-CDs) using oleic acid as the reaction medium, ʟ-cystine and citric acid monohydrate as raw materials. In addition, we employ the “on-off” method and found that the fluorescence of NS CDs was quenched by Fe3+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, the addition of AA promotes the “on-off-on” response, resulting in fluorescence recovery. Finally, the detection limit in the aqueous phase was determined, and the developed method was applied for determination of the AA contents of actual test samples to demonstrate the feasibility of this method.

In addition to the instruments detailed in this section, a Sartorius electronic analytical balance (Beijing Sartorius Co., Ltd., China), a Millipore MINGCHE D24UV integral water purification system for ultrapure water (Millipore, USA), a DZF-6050 vacuum drying chamber (Shanghai Boxon Industry Co., Ltd., China), a KQ3200E ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Chaosheng Instrument Co., Ltd., China), a Vario-EL elemental analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany), a ZF-7 dark-box triple-use UV analyzer (Shenglan Instrument Manufacturing Co., Ltd., China), and a UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) were used.

ʟ-Cysteine (AR, Tianjin AlfaAesar Chemical Co., Ltd., China), AA (AR, Xilong Chemicals Co., Ltd., China), oleic acid, hexane (AR, Xilong Chemicals Shantou Dahao Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd., China), quinine sulfate, laboratory ultrapure water (18.2 MΩcm), and all other reagents employed herein were of analytical grade with a purity of >99%.

NS-CDs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method. More specifically, 5 g ʟ-cystine and 5 g citric acid monohydrate were mixed in a 150-mL three-necked flask with a mass ratio of 1:1. Oleic acid (40 mL) was then added, and the resulting mixture stirred magnetically at 1200 rpm for 30 min at 220 °C. After cooling to 30 °C, the precipitated product was washed with n-hexane, dissolved in ultrapure water, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was dried under vacuum and stored in an Ampere bottle at normal atmospheric temperature.

A Tecnai-G2 F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Company, USA) was used to characterize the NS-CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the nanoparticles were recorded on a Bruker DAVINCI D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS GMBX, Germany). Infrared spectra were measured using a Magna-IR-750 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). A Thermo-ESCALAB-250XI multi-function imaging photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–visible spectra were recorded using a UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer. A Cary Edipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) was used to collect the fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of the NS-CDs was measured according to a previously established procedure.[23] Specifically, a solution of quinine sulfate in a 0.1 M solution of H2SO4 (refractive index η = 1.33) with a reported quantum yield (
φ R
) of 0.54 at 340 nm was used as the standard. The NS-CDs were dissolved in ultrapure water (refractive index η = 1.33), and the UV absorption values and integral fluorescence intensities of the standard and the analyte (the absorbance of the solution should be <0.1) were measured under the same excitation conditions. Standard curves of the absorbance-fluorescence integral area for both the standard and the analyte were then plotted. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
φ =
φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield of the sample, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength, A is the UV absorbance, η represents the refractive index of the solvent (in aqueous solution, η/ηR = 1), and the subscript R denotes the standard with a known fluorescence quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and AA using the NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NS-CDs were dissolved in ultrapure water to obtain a 100 μg/mL nanoparticle solution. The NS-CDs solution and various concentrations of Fe3+ (1–20 μm) and AA (1–40 μm) solutions were then added sequentially to a 10-mL colorimetric tube. After setting the volume of ultrapure water to 20 mL, the fluorescence spectrum was measured (340 nm excitation wavelength, 350–650 nm scan range).

According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the limit of detection is expressed in terms of concentration (or mass) and refers to the lowest concentration (or mass) determined by the smallest analytical signal L that can be detected by a certain analytical procedure (see Eq. (2)):
(2)
L =
KS b
/ S
where the recommended value of K is 3, L represents the lowest detected concentration or mass of the analytical method, Sb is the standard deviation (absorbance) of multiple measurements of the blank sample, and S represents the sensitivity of the method (i.e., the slope of the calibration correction curve). Blank measurements were recorded at least 20 times.

The NS-CDs were synthesized in oleic acid medium, using citric acid monohydrate and ʟ-cysteine as precursors. As shown in Fig. 1 (A) and (B), the NS-CDs were prepared as relatively uniform spherical particles. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1C) exhibited an inter-planar crystal spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the {100} facet of graphene.[24]. The XRD spectrum of the NS-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(D), where the characteristic absorption peak at 27.5° corresponds to literature value for fluorescent carbon particles.[11,16] This indicates that the NS-CDs adopted a degree of crystallinity. In addition, we note that the NS-CDs contain a small amount of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, resulting in moderate water solubility.

The FTIR spectrum of the NS-CDs is shown in Fig. 2 . The peak at 3332.2 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H bond stretching vibrations, while the intense signals at 2855.8 and 2923.13 cm−1 indicate the presence of –CH2– groups, which in turn confirms the chain structure of the NS-CDs. In addition, the signals at 1652.43 and 1455.58 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of the C=O, C–N, and C–S moieties, and the characteristic peak at 718.92 cm−1 was attributed to the respiratory vibration peak of the triazine ring.

To further determine the chemical structure of the NS-CDs, XPS was conducted. The obtained results (Fig. 3 ) show that the NS-CNP surface is primarily composed of carbon (48.47%), nitrogen (17.03%), oxygen (19.65%), and sulfur (14.85%), with the relatively high C content indicating that the surface was highly carbonized. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 3(A), four main peaks exist in the XPS spectrum, corresponding to C1s (284.61 eV), N1s (400.26 eV), O1s (528.18 eV), and S2p (163.49 eV). In addition, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3(B)) can be broken down into signals at 284.61, 285.22, 286.12, 287.97, and 288.68 eV, corresponding to sp2 C=C, C=O, and N–C=N bonds, or sp3 C–C, C–N, C–O, and C–S bonds. Similarly, a breakdown of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3(C)) confirms the presence of three chemical bonds, i.e., C–N–C (400.26 eV), N–(C)3 (400.92 eV), and N–H (401.58 eV). Furthermore, the S2p spectrum (Fig. 3(D)) exhibits two peaks, corresponding to C–S–C (163.49 eV) and S–(C)3 (164.71 eV), respectively.

The optical properties of the NS-CDs were then examined by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrometry. Fig. 4 (A) shows that absorption peaks were observed at 240 and 345 nm. The former corresponds to the characteristic peak of the fluorescent CDs, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of electrons in the triazine structure. In contrast, the absorption peak at 345 nm is characteristic of a typical fluorescent nanoparticle.[11,16] The fluorescence spectra of the NS-CDs conformed to the UV adsorption results. Specifically, the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of the NS-CDs were 340 and 420 nm, respectively. We also note that an aqueous solution of the NS-CDs was transparent at fluorescence and appeared blue under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset, Fig. 4(A)).

Fig. 4(B) shows the spectra obtained when excited between 300 and 400 nm. As shown in the figure, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak reached a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm prior to decreasing gradually at higher wavelengths. This is because the fluorescence of the product mainly arises from group emission, in this case, the functional group is relatively simple. Changing the excitation wavelength can only change the intensity of the emission wavelength without changing its position.
Meanwhile, the quantum yield (QY) of the obtained N,S-CDs is calculated to be 23.7% using quinine sulfate as a reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the Fe3+ and AA contents using the NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of reaction time, pH, common ions, and amino acids on the fluorescence intensity were investigated. To understand the response speed of the NS-CDs in the detection of Fe3+ and AA, the intensities of the fluorescence signals were studied within the detection system over time.

Thus, as indicated by the black line (squares) in Fig. 5 , when 20 μ m Fe3+ is added, fluorescence was quenched rapidly (82.43% within one minute) at 360 nm, and the quench rate reaching 88.84% after 15 min. The fluorescence intensity remained relatively constant after 20 min, indicating that Fe3+ quenching was rapid and stable. In addition, the red line (circles) shown in Fig. 5 indicates that fluorescence at 360 nm was rapidly restored within 1 min following the addition of 40 μM AA, with a recovery rate of 76.4%. After 5 min, the fluorescence intensity changed little, indicating that the fluorescence intensity recovered quickly and stably after adding AA.

The effects of pH on the fluorescence quenching and the fluorescence recovery of the NS-CDs are outlined in Fig. 6 . As indicated, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively constant between pH 3 and 10, while above pH 11, the fluorescence intensity decreased only slightly. The effect of pH on the systems containing Fe3+, AA, and Fe3+-AA were similar, indicating that pH had little effect on these three systems. Since the maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved at pH 7 in all the cases, pH 7 was employed for all subsequent experiments.

Solutions containing a series of common ions were added to the NS-CNP solution, and the fluorescence selectivity of the fluorescent CDs was examined by scanning fluorescence spectroscopy at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (see Fig. 7 ). The results show that Fe3+ can obviously quench the fluorescence, and the quenching rate is 82.43%. However, other common ions, such as K+ and Na+, had negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CDs, indicating that they were selective toward Fe3+ ion.

Upon comparison of Fig. 7(A) and (C), it is apparent that the fluorescence of the NS-CDs was quenched only slightly upon the addition of AA. In addition, examination of Fig. 7(B), (C), and (D) shows that the addition of AA recovers the fluorescence of the NS-CDs + Fe3+ system, but does not restore it to the intensity shown in Fig. 7(A) probably due to some degree of fluorescence quenching by AA.

The fluorescence recovery of the NS-CDs + Fe3+ system was then examined in the presence of other amino acids. However, as shown in Fig. 8 , other common amino acids, such as Gly, Val, Phe, Ala, His, and Trp, failed to restore the fluorescence of the NS-CDs + Fe3+ system, indicating that AA exhibits good selectivity for this system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the NS-CDs to the detection of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensitivity of the NS-CDs as ferric ion sensors, the linear response range of the NS-CDs was measured in the detection of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 9 (A), with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CDs at 340 nm decreased gradually. Indeed, this fluorescence quenching showed a linear relationship with ferric ion concentrations of 0–10 μM, giving a linear correlation coefficient of 0.99967. We also note that a detection limit of 36.6 nM was obtained, and the sensitivity was high.

Then we further determined the linear response range of the reversal of fluorescence quenching in the NS-CDs-Fe3+ system by AA. In this case, with the increase of AA concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CDs-Fe3+ emission peak at 340 nm gradually increased (Fig. 10 (A)). The fluorescence recovery rate also showed a linear relationship with AA concentrations of 0–30 μM. A linear correlation coefficient of 0.99818 was obtained, and the detection limit was 102.5 nM.

After confirmation the selectivity of our system, we moved on to examine its application in the measurement of Fe3+ in three water samples containing known Fe3+ concentrations (i.e. 0.2 μg·mL−1). As indicated in Table 1 , the recovery ranged from 92.3 to 98.8%, indicating that this method exhibited good recovery rate of Fe3+. In addition, the relative standard deviation for the experimental data was less than 4.35%, which shows that the method has high accuracy. Therefore, it was apparent that this method exhibits potential for the detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution.

To further investigate the applicability of this method, three types of vitamin C tablets were purchased from pharmacies, and their AA concentrations were determined, as outlined in Table 2 . Samples 1–3 (2.5, 2.5, and 5.78 mg, respectively) were ground and fully dissolved in ultrapure water in a 250-mL volumetric flask. The AA contents of these samples were measured using our described method, and values of 2.32, 2.17, and 5.61 mg were obtained for the samples 1, 2, and 3, respectively. These values were close to the masses employed and so it was apparent that our method was potentially suitable for the determination of AA concentrations.

We herein reported the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (NS-CDs) via a facile and rapid one-step solvothermal method using ʟ-cystine and citric acid monohydrate as precursors. This system is of particular interest because these raw materials are readily available and inexpensive, and the obtained product exhibits high fluorescence quantum yield. Based on the “on–off-on” fluorescence quenching mode, we found that the synthesized nanoparticles could be employed as fluorescent chemical sensors to directly detect free iron (Fe3+) in aqueous solution, and to indirectly determine the ascorbic acid (AA) contents of vitamin C samples, thereby constituting a novel method for AA detection. Furthermore, we demonstrated that this system exhibits good selectivity and linearity within the concentration ranges of 0–10 μM (Fe3+, n = 3) and 0–30 μM (AA, n = 3). Finally, we concluded that our reported system can be applied in the detection of both Fe3+ and AA in actual test samples.

Yujing Nie: Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Jiaqing Guo: Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Yanhua Deng: Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Wen Weng: Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Fujian Province of China [grant number 2018H0030], and the Education Bureau of the Fujian Province of China [grant number JZ160453].

Z X Gan, Y L Hao and Y Shan. ChemNanoMat 2016,2:171-175
